data.title: ‘Annoyance Economy’ costs U.S. households $165B a year, study finds
data.description:
The everyday frustrations of modern life such as being stuck on hold, dodging spam calls or navigating maze-like cancellation processes may seem minor, but their cumulative impact is far from trivial.

A new study by Neale Mahoney of Stanford University and Chad Maisel at Groundwork Collaborative estimates that these hassles cost American households about $165 billion annually in lost time and unnecessary expenses, the New York Times reported Sunday.

The researchers describe this phenomenon as the “annoyance economy,” referring to routine interactions that should be straightforward but often become unnecessarily complicated. These include dealing with hidden fees, insurance claims, robocalls and difficult subscription cancellations.

While some inefficiencies stem from outdated systems or regulation, the study suggests others may be deliberately designed to benefit companies. Businesses that make it harder for customers to cancel services, for example, can see revenue increases ranging from 14% to 200%.

The rise of artificial intelligence may intensify the problem, as scammers become more adept at using the technology, the report warns.

Beyond financial costs, these obstacles can also affect well-being. Prior research shows that administrative burdens such as scheduling appointments or resolving billing issues can lead people to delay or skip medical care.

Public frustration with these issues is widespread. Polling indicates strong support for tighter restrictions on robocalls and broader consumer protections, suggesting growing pressure on policymakers to address the problem.

data.title: Gulf allies rush to rebuild air defenses after Iran strikes expose gaps
data.description:
After weeks of intense air attacks depleted regional defenses, U.S. allies in the Middle East are racing to replenish missile systems and counter new threats, particularly cheap drones, The Wall Street Journal reported Sunday.

Countries including Saudi Arabia, Qatar and the United Arab Emirates are expanding beyond traditional U.S. suppliers, exploring South Korean missile systems, Ukrainian interceptor drones and even rapid-fire guns as quicker, lower-cost solutions.

The urgency reflects how unprepared the region was for the scale of Iran’s retaliatory strikes, as well as the growing effectiveness of low-cost drone warfare. At the same time, global arms production, especially in the United States, is struggling to keep pace with demand, leaving some orders delayed by years.

Gulf states are also pursuing defense partnerships with Ukraine and engaging newer suppliers, including startups offering cheaper anti-drone technologies. But even these sources face supply constraints, as Ukraine prioritizes its own war needs.

With traditional systems like Patriot missiles in short supply, defense officials are increasingly focused on what can be delivered quickly. The scramble highlights a broader shift: in a world of prolonged conflict, speed and adaptability may matter more than relying solely on established, high-end weapons systems.

data.title: Prediction markets surge, but Wall Street giants remain on sidelines
data.description:
Trading in prediction markets is booming, with platforms like Polymarket (POLYMARKET) and Kalshi (KALSHI) handling billions of dollars in weekly wagers tied to everything from sports to geopolitics, Bloomberg News reported Sunday.

Despite the rapid growth, major trading firms such as Citadel Securities, IMC Trading and Hudson River Trading have largely stayed out. Regulatory uncertainty and relatively small market size are part of the hesitation, but structural challenges also play a role.

Unlike traditional markets, event-based contracts lack an underlying asset, making it difficult for traders to hedge risk using standard strategies. As one researcher cited by Bloomberg News said, “Unlike options or futures markets, event contracts do not reference an underlying spot asset that permits mechanical hedging.”

This limitation forces participants to take direct bets on outcomes, more akin to insurance or credit underwriting than conventional market-making. Without effective hedging tools, firms often rely on wider spreads to manage risk. In some cases, these margins far exceed those seen in traditional financial markets.

While some firms, including Susquehanna International Group, have begun exploring the space, leveraging experience in sports betting, the broader industry appears to be waiting for more mature risk-transfer mechanisms before committing significant capital.

data.title: IMF’s Georgieva warns war fallout will linger as global growth outlook dims
data.description:
Global prices are unlikely to quickly return to pre-war levels following the U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran, even if a ceasefire holds, according to International Monetary Fund Managing Director Kristalina Georgieva.

“It will take some time, yes, and it will take more time for locations that are experiencing higher degree of disruption,” Georgieva said in remarks aired Sunday on CBS’s Face the Nation ahead of the IMF and World Bank spring meetings. “That’s why we need to remember the asymmetry of this shock.”

She emphasized that the economic impact of the conflict will not be evenly distributed, with some regions facing more prolonged disruptions than others.

Georgieva also confirmed that the IMF plans to cut its global growth outlook due to the war’s effects on production and supply chains.

“We are going to have a downgrade, and the size of this downgrade will depend on these two things, duration and speed with which everything can come back to the same level of production that we had before,” she said.

Her comments come as geopolitical tensions continue to escalate. President Donald Trump said Sunday that the U.S. will impose a full naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, a key artery for global energy shipments, and warned of retaliation if Iran resists.

The announcement followed unsuccessful direct negotiations between U.S. and Iranian officials in Pakistan, casting doubt on whether a recently declared ceasefire will hold or lead to a lasting resolution.

data.title: Airlines cut global schedules as Iran-driven energy shock ripples into May
data.description:
Airlines are scaling back flight schedules more aggressively as disruptions tied to the Iran conflict continue to constrain energy flows, according to new research from BNP Paribas.

Analyst Matt Akers said in a note to clients on Friday that carriers have reduced global capacity for April by roughly 7%, a deeper pullback than the 5% cut estimated just a week earlier. May schedules are also starting to weaken, now running about 2% below pre-conflict levels, compared with a marginal decline previously.

The data suggests that the impact on air travel is becoming more pronounced as elevated fuel costs and operational uncertainty weigh on airline planning.

The Middle East remains the epicenter of the disruption. Flight schedules in the region are down sharply, with April capacity off by about 43% compared with pre-conflict plans, and May schedules currently trailing by around 14%. Major regional hubs are still operating at roughly one-third below normal flight volumes, with little improvement in recent days.

Outside the Middle East, reductions are more moderate but still broadening. Asian carriers have trimmed April schedules by nearly 5% and May by just under 1%, while European airlines are down about 4% for April and slightly below 1% for May. In North America, cuts have been relatively limited, with capacity reductions of roughly 2% for both months.

Akers noted that if fuel prices remain elevated, airlines may begin accelerating the retirement of older, less efficient aircraft, which become increasingly uneconomical to operate in a high-cost environment. However, historical precedent suggests oil price spikes have not always translated into sustained damage for the industry.

For now, demand appears resilient. Recent checks with airlines in late March indicated little change in passenger behavior, even as geopolitical tensions intensified and operating costs climbed.

Still, the widening gap between pre-conflict schedules and current capacity plans underscores how quickly geopolitical shocks can ripple through global aviation, particularly when they intersect with energy markets.

data.title: Leonard Green nears $3B deal for Cumming Group amid sluggish PE market
data.description:
Buyout firm Leonard Green & Partners is reportedly close to acquiring Cumming Group from New Mountain Capital, in a transaction that could value the business at around $3 billion including debt.

The deal, which could be announced within weeks, would transfer ownership of a consultancy known for advising on major infrastructure developments worldwide, including the multibillion-dollar overhaul of Terminal One at John F. Kennedy International Airport.

The potential acquisition comes against a challenging backdrop for private equity firms, as higher borrowing costs, rapid advances in artificial intelligence and geopolitical instability weigh on dealmaking activity. Industry data indicates that exits backed by private equity have dropped significantly this year, reflecting a more difficult environment for sellers.

For New Mountain, the transaction would mark one of its largest divestments in recent years, following a decision to shelve plans to sell a group of healthcare technology assets.

Leonard Green has remained active despite the slowdown, recently taking a majority position in the Topgolf and Toptracer business from Topgolf Callaway Brands Corp. in a deal that valued the unit at about $1.1 billion.

data.title: Oil shock rekindles 1970s fears, but today’s economy is better prepared
data.description:
The global economy is facing a jarring sense of déjà vu, as rising oil prices driven by conflict in the Middle East revive concerns reminiscent of the 1970s energy crises, the Associated Press reported Sunday.

The surge in energy costs affecting gasoline, diesel and jet fuel has renewed worries about stagflation, the painful combination of slow growth and persistent inflation that defined that earlier era.

Yet economists and energy experts say the current situation, while serious, is not an exact replay. Governments and industries have spent decades adapting to energy shocks, leaving today’s economy more resilient than it was half a century ago.

“We have decades of experience now dealing with these kinds of oil shocks,’’ said Amy Myers Jaffe, research professor at New York University’s Center for Global Affairs.

The present disruption stems from the conflict involving Iran, which has effectively halted much of the flow through the Strait of Hormuz—a key chokepoint that typically carries about one-fifth of global oil supply. While some shipments have been rerouted, a significant portion of daily output remains offline, making the shock larger in scale than past disruptions such as the 1973 embargo.

Despite the magnitude, structural changes in the global energy system are helping to soften the blow. Oil now accounts for a smaller share of total energy consumption than it did in the 1970s, with natural gas, nuclear power and renewables playing a much larger role.

The United States, in particular, has undergone a dramatic transformation. Once heavily dependent on imports, the country has boosted domestic production through advances such as hydraulic fracturing, eventually becoming a net exporter of petroleum.

“The U.S. economy is much better positioned than it was in the 1970s,” when it was “particularly vulnerable to an oil price shock,” said Sam Ori, executive director of the University of Chicago’s Energy Policy Institute.

Energy use patterns have also shifted. In the early 1970s, oil played a significant role in electricity generation in the U.S., but policy changes have largely eliminated that reliance. Today, petroleum is used almost exclusively in transportation, rather than power production.

The oil crises of the past also prompted sweeping efficiency measures. Fuel economy standards have improved significantly, and many countries diversified energy sources while building strategic reserves to buffer future disruptions.

Central banks, too, have learned from history. Policymakers in the 1970s cut interest rates to support growth, but underestimated inflation risks—a mistake modern officials are keen to avoid. As one analysis noted, “What we can learn from the 1970s is that a well-intentioned policy of stimulating the economy by lowering interest rates has the potential of inadvertently reigniting inflation.’’

Even so, vulnerabilities remain. Transportation systems worldwide still rely heavily on petroleum, meaning that price shocks can quickly ripple through the broader economy.

“OIl is still king, the No. 1 fuel in the U.S. economy,” Ori said, adding that disruptions in any part of the global supply chain can drive up costs everywhere.

He also warned that recent policy shifts could leave the U.S. more exposed in the future. Measures aimed at reducing dependence on oil, including incentives for electric vehicles and stricter fuel efficiency standards, are being rolled back, potentially reversing some of the progress made since the 1970s.

While today’s energy shock is unlikely to produce the same shortages and rationing seen decades ago, the episode serves as a reminder that geopolitical conflict and oil markets remain tightly intertwined, and that the lessons of the past are still being tested.

data.title: Box office buzz: Best quarter in five years fuels hopes for Hollywood comeback
data.description:
The U.S. box office sizzled in Q1, with revenue climbing 25% year over year to $1.8B and surpassing analysts’ expectations. Notably, the quarterly tally marked the highest in five years, though it remains below pre-pandemic levels, according to analysis from Macquarie.

Leading the Q1 results were Sony Pictures’ (SONY) Project Hail Mary ($177M), continued momentum from 20th Century Studios’ (FOX) Avatar: Fire and Ash ($154M), and Illumination/Universal’s (CMCSA) Hoppers ($142M).

Looking ahead, Q2 began on a strong note with Nintendo (NTDOY) and Universal’s (CMCSA) The Super Mario Galaxy Movie, which opened April 1 and has already grossed over $200M domestically. The momentum could continue through an eventful slate that includes Lionsgate’s (LION) Michael (April 24), 20th Century Studios’ (DIS) The Devil Wears Prada 2 (May 1), Lucasfilm's (DIS) The Mandalorian and Grogu (May 22), Paramount's (PSKY)  Disclosure Day (June 12), Pixar's (DIS) Toy Story 5 (June 19), and Warner Bros.' (WBD) Supergirl (June 26).

This week, the buzz in the film industry will be surrounding CinemaCon on April 13-16 in Las Vegas. Macquarie analyst Chad Beynon and his team expect key topics to be studio consolidation, the 45-day theatrical window amid Netflix (NFLX) pressure, the encouraging film slate, and capital expenditure spending.

"Theatre company management teams continue to be positive on box office growth in '26 given the strength of the film slate and ongoing demand for premium formats, which should benefit average ticket price (ATP). Additionally, F&B and merchandise sales continue to see positive trends as well," highlighted Beynon.

The firm maintained its $9.7B industry box office forecast for the full year, which would be the best mark for the industry since 2019.

data.title: Saudi Arabia restores key oil pipeline capacity as Red Sea exports surge
data.description:
Saudi Arabia returned its East-West pipeline to full operating capacity of about 7 million barrels a day, reestablishing a critical route for crude shipments to the Red Sea.

The pipeline had been partially disrupted after a recent strike damaged one of its 11 pumping stations along the 746-mile network, cutting flows by roughly 700,000 barrels a day. The incident came shortly after a ceasefire was announced in the Iran conflict.

To offset severe constraints at the Strait of Hormuz, the kingdom has significantly increased shipments from its Red Sea export terminals, with volumes rising sharply since late February.

According to the energy ministry, production at Saudi Aramco’s offshore Manifa field has now been restored. However, repair work is still underway at the Khurais onshore facility. Both sites had been impacted by earlier attacks, each losing about 300,000 barrels per day of output capacity.

Officials said the rapid recovery underscores the resilience of Saudi Arabia’s energy infrastructure and its ability to maintain supply continuity despite regional instability.

Khurais primarily produces lighter grades of crude that are typically transported through the East-West pipeline, while Manifa and other offshore fields tend to produce heavier oil varieties.

data.title: U.K. regulators race to assess risks from Anthropic’s powerful new AI model: FT
data.description:
U.K. financial authorities are moving quickly to evaluate potential threats linked to a newly unveiled artificial intelligence system from Anthropic (ANTHRO), coordinating with government cybersecurity officials and major banks, the Financial Times reported Sunday, citing people familiar with the discussions.

Representatives from the Bank of England, Financial Conduct Authority and HM Treasury are working alongside the National Cyber Security Centre to analyze weaknesses in critical systems that the model may have exposed.

Senior figures from leading British banks, insurers and market infrastructure firms are expected to receive briefings within the next two weeks on risks associated with the experimental system, known as Claude Mythos Preview.

The U.K. response follows similar moves in the United States, where Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent recently convened top Wall Street executives to examine the model’s ability to uncover software vulnerabilities that could potentially be exploited.

Anthropic (ANTHRO) has said the system has already identified thousands of serious flaws across operating systems, web browsers and other widely used technologies, including some that had remained undetected for years. The company warned that such capabilities could soon become more widely available, raising concerns about misuse and broader risks to economies and national security.

The issue is expected to be discussed at the next meeting of the U.K.’s Cross Market Operational Resilience Group, a forum that brings together regulators and financial firms to address systemic threats. The group is co-led by Duncan Mackinnon and David Postings, and includes representatives from major banks, insurers and financial infrastructure providers.

Industry groups say they are monitoring the situation closely. David Raw said the organization is engaging with its members and public-sector partners to evaluate any operational risks that could affect the resilience of the financial system.

While the Bank of England has mechanisms to convene emergency meetings with financial institutions at short notice, it has not yet taken that step in response to the AI-related concerns.

The developments come amid a broader rise in cyber threats. Several large U.K. companies, including Marks & Spencer, Co-op Group, Harrods and Jaguar Land Rover, were hit by disruptive cyberattacks last year.

Meanwhile, the U.K.’s AI Safety Institute is testing Anthropic’s system alongside other leading models such as those from OpenAI (OPENAI). Policymakers are also considering standardized testing requirements for AI systems used by banks, following earlier warnings from regulators that firms were not reviewing their models frequently enough.
